Arabia. Prophet Muhammad established Islam. He was born around AD in Mecca. In AD, he completed the Hegira, his migration from Mecca to Medina, to escape persecution. Q.185. The decline of the Gupta Empire led to the rise of various ruling dynasties across northern India. Among the most notable were the Pushyabhutis of Thanesar, the Maukhari of Kanauj, and the Maitrakas of Valabhi.
Kushana. Vasudeva I was the last of the "Great Kushans." He ruled in Northern India and Central Asia, where he minted coins in the city of Balkh. The Kushana dynasty was founded by Kujula Kadphises.

Ramapala was the successor to the Pala king Shurapala II in the Bengal region of the Indian subcontinent, and 15th ruler of the Pala line. Sandhyakar Nandi was patronaged by Madanapala, ruler of Pala Dynasty.

Vakataka dynasty originated from the Deccan in the mid-3rd century CE. Vakatakas were the most important successors of the Satavahanas in the Deccan and coexistent with the Guptas in northern India. Q.190. An inscription, Takht-i-Bahi recovered from Mardan near Peshawar, indicates the rule of which of the following dynasties in north-western area of present Pakistan? SSC CGL 17/09/2024 (1st Shift) (a) Bactrian (b) Sakas (c) Indo-Greek (d) Parthians Sol.190.(d) Parthians (247 BC - 224 AD). It was founded by Arsaces I of Parthia when he rebelled against the Seleucid Empire. They replaced the Sakas in North-Western India. The Takht-i-Bahi inscription, dated in 45 AD, refers to Gondophernes as a Parthian ruler.
Kannauj. The Maukharis, initially vassals of the Guptas and titled Samantas, gained independence in the mid-6th century as the Gupta Empire declined.

Dharmapala, the second ruler of the Pala Dynasty, ascended the throne around AD after Gopala and expanded the empire significantly. He defeated Indrayudha of Kanauj, installing Chakrayudha as his nominee, but later faced defeat by Rashtrakuta king Dhruva near Monghyr, Bihar.

Kannada Literature. Pampa, also known as Adi Pampa, is renowned for his literary works, including "Vikramarjuna Vijaya" and "Adi Purana." Ponna, often referred to as Ponna Kavishwara, served as a court poet for Rashtrakuta king Krishna III and is best known for his epic "Shantipurana." Ranna was a court poet of the Western Chalukya Empire, and his most celebrated work is "Gadayuddha."

Satavahana : Period : 60 BC - 225 AD; Capital - Pratishthana (Paithan) and Amravati; Founder - Simuka. The lowest level of administration was a grama which was under the charge of a Gramika. There were also officers called amatyas who were perhaps ministers or advisors of the king. Revenue was collected both in cash and kind. It majorly comprised present Andhra Pradesh, Telangana, and Maharashtra.

Tolkappiyam. Tamil has a literary tradition that goes back to the period of the Sangams. The most well-known surviving works from this period are the Tolkappiyam (a book of grammar attributed to Tolkappiyar), Ettu Togai (Eight Anthologies), Pattu Pattu (Ten Idylls).

Madurai. Megasthenes: A Greek diplomat and historian who mentioned the Pandya Kingdom in his book Indica around 300 BCE. The Pandya Kingdom was an ancient Tamil kingdom that ruled parts of southern India, especially Tamil Nadu, from the 6th century BCE to the 15th century CE.

Vakatakas. Vindhyashakti founded the Vakataka dynasty in the third century. Their history is mainly known through land grant charters issued to Brahmanas. The Maukhari dynasty ruled over (modern day) Uttar Pradesh and parts of Bihar.

Kashmir. Didda was the ruler of Kashmir from 980 CE to 1003 CE. Most knowledge relating to Didda is obtained from the Rajatarangini. Ashish Kaul's novel Didda: The Warrior Queen of Kashmir is based on Didda's life.

Pallava dynasty (275 CE to 897 CE) - They ruled a significant portion of the Deccan, also known as Tondaimandalam, which covered parts of present-day Tamil Nadu and Andhra Pradesh. Founder - Simha Vishnu (Avanisimha).

Nagabhatta I (Reign : 730 - AD) laid the foundation of the Pratihara dynasty's power. He is best known for defeating the Arabs during their expansion and building an empire that stretched from Gujarat to Gwalior, stopping further Arab invasions in eastern Sindh. Q.201. Pushyamitra Shunga founded the Shunga Empire after assassinating the last Mauryan emperor, Brihadratha Maurya. He ruled from 184 BC to 75 BC, driving out the Greeks through the Shunga-Greek war. The capital of the Shunga Empire was Pataliputra.

Narasimhavarman. Pulakesin II, a ruler of the Chalukya dynasty, reigned from 610 CE to 642 CE, expanding his empire across much of the Deccan region. However, in 642-643 CE, Pallava king Narasimha Varman I defeated Pulakesin II when his general, Shiruttondar Paranjoti, captured the Chalukya capital, Vatapi. Simhavishnu is regarded as the founder of the Pallava dynasty, a Tamil dynasty that ruled parts of South India from the 3rd to the 9th century.

The Kushan Empire was a syncretic empire established by the Yuezhi in the Bactrian territories in the early 1st century. The Mathura School of Art flourished under the Kushana emperor Kanishka during the first century AD.

The Kadambas ruled from Banavasi in present-day Karnataka, marking a significant period in the early medieval history of the Deccan region. Badami was the capital of the early Chalukyan dynasty, which governed the area from 543 to 598 CE. With the decline of the Vakataka rule, the Chalukyas established their dominance in the Deccan. Dharmapala. The Tripartite Struggle, also known as the Kannauj Triangle Wars, took place in the 8th and 9th centuries, between the three dynasties - The Palas of Bengal, The Pratiharas of Central India and The Rashtrakutas of Deccan. Q.207. The founder of the Pallava dynasty was: SSC CHSL 09/07/2024 (1st shift) (a) Balasimha (b) Simharaj (c) Singhamvishnu (d) Simhavishnu Sol.207.(d) Simhavishnu (575-590 AD). He was the first Pallava monarch whose domain extended beyond Kanchipuram (Kanchi) in the South. Rise : Defeating Kalabhras inaugurated the rule of imperial Pallavas. Royal Titles: Avanisimha (lion of the Earth). His court poet Bharavi - Wrote Kiratarjuniya.

Vikramaditya VI. The Chalukya dynasty was established by Pulakeshin I in 543. He took Vatapi (modern Badami in Bagalkot district, Karnataka) under his control and made it his capital. He and his descendants are referred to as "Chalukyas of Badami".
Korkai. Pandya dynasty (4th century BCE to the 17th century CE): Capital - Madurai, Founder - Mudathirumaran.

Rashtrakuta. Manyakheta was the capital of the Rashtrakuta kingdom. The Elichpur clan, initially a feudatory of the Badami Chalukyas, rose to power under Dantidurga by overthrowing Chalukya ruler Kirtivarman II and establishing an empire in the Gulbarga region. This clan became known as the Rashtrakutas of Manyakheta, who emerged as a significant power in Maharashtra-Karnataka region.

Rameswaram. Krishna III whose Kannada name was Kannara (939 – 967 C.E.) was the last great warrior and able monarch of the Rashtrakuta dynasty {Founder: Dantivarman}. Its Capital - Manyakheta. Parantaka I was known as the destroyer of Madurai (the capital city of the Pandyas).

Nanniah (Nannayya) is regarded as the first poet of Telugu literature and is part of the famous "Trinity of Poets" (comprising Nannaya, Tikkana and Erra Pragada) in Telugu literature. He began the translation of the Mahabharata from Sanskrit into Telugu during the 11th century under the patronage of the Eastern Chalukya king, Rajaraja Narendra.
Rashtrakuta. Dantidurga was the founder of the Rashtrakuta empire who fixed his capital at Manyakheta or Malkhed near modern Sholapur.
Narasimhavarman II, also known as Rajasimha, ruled the Pallava dynasty from 700 to 728 CE. He also constructed the Kailasanatha Temple in Kanchipuram.

Sanskrit. The word "Rajatarangini" means "River of Kings." This book chronicles the history of the northwestern Indian subcontinent, specifically the kings of Kashmir. It contains 7,826 verses divided into eight sections known as Tarangas (waves).

Nagabhatta I. The Gurjara - Pratiharas Dynasty (8th-11th Century A.D.) ruled over northern and western India. Gautamiputra Satakarni. He is renowned for his military conquests and is described in the Nasik inscription as the "destroyer of the Sakas, Yavanas, and Pahlavas." Senas Dynasty: A hindu dynasty that ruled over eastern Bengal and western Bihar for about two centuries. Important Rulers - Vijaya Sena, Ballal Sena, Lakshmana Sena. Deva dynasty - Ruled over eastern Bengal after the Sena dynasty.

Bharhut and Amravati. Bharhut Stupa is located in Satna, Madhya Pradesh. It was discovered in a ruined state by Alexander Cunningham and his team. The discovered railing and gateway are now on display at the Indian Museum (Kolkata). Amravati Stupa was discovered by Colin Mackenzie (1797).

Astronomy. Brihat Samhita - It is the noted work of Varahamihira. It is an important encyclopedic text in Sanskrit. Other Samhitas - Jaya Samhita (Mahabharat), Charak Samhita (Medicinal Science), Sushruta Samhita (Book on Surgeries and related science).

Ajayaraja. Chahamanas or Chauhans dynasty: Chauhans of Delhi and Ajmer, also known as the Chahamanas of Shakambhari, ruled over part of modern-day Rajasthan. Important Kings : Vasudeva, Ajayaraja, Arnoraja, Vigraharaja IV, Prithviraj Chauhan III, and Hariraja.

Chand Bardai. Indian Epics and writers - Mahabharata (Sage Ved Vyasa), Ramayana (Valmiki), Raghuvamsa (Kalidasa), Buddhacharita (Asvogosha), Silapadikaram (Ilango Adigal in Tamil), Ramcharitmanas (Tulsidas in Awadhi), Kumarasambava (Kalidasa in Sanskrit). Banabhatta wrote Harshacharita and Bharavi wrote Kiratarjuniya.

Pala Dynasty. Temples and their associated Dynasty: Khajuraho Temple (Madhya Pradesh) - Bundelas or Chandelas; Kailash Temple (Maharashtra) - Rashtrakutas; Thousand Pillared Temple, Ramappa Temple (Telangana) - Kakatiyas; Brihadeeswara Temple (Tamil Nadu) - Cholas; Kailashnath Temple, Shore Temple (Tamil Nadu) - Pallavas; Dilwara Temple (Rajasthan) - Solankies; Virupaksha Temple (Karnataka) - Chalukyas; Sun Temple (Gujarat) - Solankis.

Kalidasa. He was a court poet of Chandragupta-II (Vikramaditya). Other Books: Meghaduta, Kumarasambhava, Raghuvaṃsa, and Vikramorvasiya. Harisena - Court poet of Samudragupta and composed Prayag pillar inscription in Sanskrit. Bhavabhuti - A great playwright, best known for his play Uttara-Ramacharitam (latter part of Rama's life). Pingala - Ancient Indian poet and mathematician, and the author of the Chandaḥsastra.
Hoysala. Hoysala Architecture Temples: Aralaguppe is the temple dedicated to Lord Channakeshava (Vishnu). The temple was built during the rule of the Hoysala Empire King Vira Someshwara.

Waihind. Hindu Shahi kingdom was founded by Kallar. It ruled over Kabul and the old province of Gandhara from the decline of the Kushan empire. This kingdom was known as the Kabul Shahi dynasty. Gahadavalas or Rathors of Kanauj (1090-1194 AD): Founder - Chandradeva. After the failure of the Pratiharas, the Gahadavalas established themselves on the throne of Kanauj. Shaktism - Devotees of Shaktism are called Shaktas. Famous Shaktas: Adi Shankaracharya, Sarada Devi, Paramahansa Yogananda.

Chandella (Founder - Nannuk). They were originally vassals of the Gurjara - Pratiharas. The Chandelas initially ruled as feudatories of the Gurjara-Pratiharas of Kanyakubja (Kannauj). Original capital - Khajuraho. The last King of this dynasty - Paradidev (Parmadi). His generals - Alha and Udal. Battle of Mahoba: Fought between Paradidev and Prithviraj Chauhan-III in 1182 AD.

Buddhist. Pala Dynasty - Founded by Gopala, in 750 AD. They were staunch supporters of Mahayana Buddhism. They were in a tripartite struggle between the Palas, the Pratiharas and the Rashtrakutas for the control of Kanauj and North India.

Shudraka. Mrichchhakatika play was translated into English by Arthur W. Ryder in 1905 as “The Little Clay Cart”. Other Sanskrit plays of Shudraka: “Vinavasavadatta”, “Padmaprabhritaka”, “a bhana” (short one -act monologue).

Devabhuti. He was assassinated by his minister (Vasudeva Kanva). Other Kings of Shunga Dynasty: Pushyamitra Shunga (Founder of Shunga Dynasty), Agnimitra, Vasujyeshtha, Vasumitra, Bhagabhadra. Shashanka: The first king of ancient Bengal, governed the Gauda Kingdom from 600 to 625 AD. Works of Rajshekhara: Viddhashalabhanjika, Balabharata, Karpuramanjari, Balaramayana, Kavyamimamsa. Gurjara Pratihara dynasty (8th to 11th century): Founder - Harichandra. Satavahanas (1st Century BC to 3rd Century AD): Simuka was the founder. Kushans (First – Third Centuries) - Founded by Kujula Kadphises.
Narasimhavarman I. He was also known as Mahamalla or Mamalla, which means "great wrestler”. He took the title Vatapikonda (the conqueror of Vatapi).

Rashtrapati Bhawan. The Pillars of Ashoka are the most famous examples of Mauryan art. The Rashtrapati Bhavan houses the imposing 3rd century BC Ashoka pillar of sandstone known as the Rampurva bull. It got its name from the place of its discovery, Rampurwa in Bihar.

Aparajitavarman. Pallava dynasty (275 CE to 897 CE) - Also known as Tondaimandalam. Founder - Simha Vishnu (Avanisimha). Mahendravarman - He defeated the Kalabhras and re-established the Pallava kingdom. Narashimhavarman-1 - Son of Mahendravarman.

Bow and Arrow Gan. The symbol also appears on the coins the dynasty minted. The dynasty controlled the central and northern parts of Kerala and the Kongu region of Tamil Nadu. Their capital was Vanji, Tamil Nadu.

Satavahanas. Different School of arts: Gandhara School of Art (Kushana) - It developed in the North West Frontier, in the modern-day Kandahar region. Mathura School of Art (Kushana) - It developed around Mathura.

Nedunjeliyan II. He was made ‘Pandya supreme in the south’. He defeated a confederacy of the Cholas and Cheras at Talaiyalanganam near Tiruvarur. His deeds have been described in the Maduraikkanci. Pandya Empire - Established by King Kadungon in the 6th century AD. Capital - Korkai, Madurai.
Three chiefs. It is a reference to the three ruling families of Tamil Nadu during the Sangam period: the Cholas, the Cheras, and the Pandyas.

Landless agricultural labourers. Terminologies related in ancient times - Shrenis: In ancient times, crafts persons and merchants formed associations.
